tests.title: IELTS Academic Reading — Test 04
tests.description: Original full-length Academic Reading test: 3 passages, 40 questions, 60 minutes.

sections.title: Passage 1 — Designing Parks for an Ageing City
sections.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 1–13.
sections.passage:
Designing Parks for an Ageing City

¶1 For most of the twentieth century, the public park was imagined chiefly as a place for the young. Planners filled it with playgrounds, football pitches and skateboard ramps, and measured its success by the laughter of children. Yet the populations of many cities are now growing older rather than younger, and a quiet rethinking of public space has begun. If a park is to serve the community that actually surrounds it, designers have started to argue, it must work just as well for a person of eighty as for a child of eight. The needs of older residents, long treated as an afterthought, are moving towards the centre of the conversation. It is a shift driven not by sentiment but by simple arithmetic, as the proportion of residents over sixty-five climbs steadily in city after city.

¶2 Those needs begin with the simple matter of getting about. An older visitor may walk more slowly, tire more quickly, or depend on a stick or a wheeled frame, and a park that ignores this can become, in effect, closed to them. Smooth, gently sloping paths matter more than dramatic flights of steps; frequent benches allow a longer walk to be broken into manageable stages; and clear, well-lit routes reduce the fear of a fall. None of these features is expensive or technically difficult, yet their absence can quietly exclude a whole section of the population from a space that is supposedly open to all. Accessibility, in this sense, is not a finishing touch but the very thing that decides whether a park exists for a particular person at all.

¶3 Comfort, however, is only the beginning. Researchers who study healthy ageing increasingly stress that the greatest threat to many older people is not physical frailty but isolation. A person who lives alone and rarely speaks to anyone faces measurable risks to both body and mind, and a well-designed park can act as a gentle remedy. Seating arranged so that strangers naturally face one another, a café that invites lingering, a community garden that needs tending, all create occasions for the unplanned conversations through which acquaintances form. The park, in this view, is valuable less as scenery than as a stage for ordinary social life.

¶4 Some cities have gone further, introducing features designed specifically with older bodies in mind. Outdoor gymnasiums, with low-impact equipment that can be used while seated, have appeared in parks from Madrid to Shanghai, allowing gentle exercise in the open air. Walking groups, often led by volunteers, turn a solitary stroll into a social occasion with a fixed time and a friendly obligation to attend. Such schemes cost little, yet studies that track their participants report improvements in mobility, mood and confidence that more expensive medical interventions struggle to match. The appeal of such schemes is that they treat company and movement as a single prescription, delivered not in a clinic but in the open air.

¶5 Designing for the old, it turns out, tends to benefit everyone else as well. A ramp installed for a wheelchair also serves a parent with a pushchair; a bench placed for a tired pensioner is equally welcome to a pregnant woman or an exhausted teenager. This principle, sometimes called inclusive design, holds that a space built to accommodate those with the greatest needs will almost always prove more comfortable for the able-bodied too. Far from being a narrow concession to one group, attention to older users raises the quality of a park for the entire community.

¶6 There are obstacles, naturally. Budgets are tight, and features aimed at older residents can be dismissed as luxuries when money is short. Older people are also, on average, less likely to attend the public meetings at which a park's future is decided, so their preferences may simply go unheard. For this reason some authorities now actively seek out the views of older residents, visiting community centres and care homes rather than waiting for those residents to come to them. Designing well for an ageing population, they have found, depends as much on listening as on landscaping.

¶7 The shift carries a deeper significance. A society reveals its values in the spaces it builds, and a city that designs its parks only for the young is, in a sense, declaring whom it expects to matter. To plan instead for a long life, with all the changing abilities that a long life entails, is to treat old age not as a problem to be hidden away but as an ordinary stage of life deserving of fresh air, company and dignity. As the world grows older, the humble park may become an unexpected test of how humanely a city can age. The benches and gentle paths, modest as they appear, are quietly redrawing the boundaries of who a city is built to hold.

questions.text: Public parks in the twentieth century were mainly designed with children in mind.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says the park 'was imagined chiefly as a place for the young', filled with playgrounds and measured by children's enjoyment.

evidence.quote: imagined chiefly as a place for the young

questions.text: Features that help older visitors move around are generally expensive and technically complex.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that 'None of these features is expensive or technically difficult', contradicting the statement.

evidence.quote: None of these features is expensive or technically difficult

questions.text: According to researchers, the biggest threat to many older people is physical frailty.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says the greatest threat 'is not physical frailty but isolation', so naming frailty as the biggest threat is false.

evidence.quote: is not physical frailty but isolation

questions.text: Outdoor gymnasiums for older people can now be found in several cities.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 says outdoor gymnasiums 'have appeared in parks from Madrid to Shanghai'.

evidence.quote: have appeared in parks from Madrid to Shanghai

questions.text: Walking groups are more effective than all medical treatments.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 says such schemes show gains that expensive interventions 'struggle to match', but it does not claim they beat all medical treatments, so this is Not Given.

questions.text: Gently sloping ______ are said to matter more for older visitors than flights of steps.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says 'Smooth, gently sloping paths matter more than dramatic flights of steps'.

evidence.quote: Smooth, gently sloping paths matter more than dramatic flights of steps

questions.text: The approach of building for those with the greatest needs is sometimes called ______ design.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 names the principle 'inclusive design'.

evidence.quote: sometimes called inclusive design

questions.text: To hear older residents' views, some authorities visit community centres and ______ homes.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 says authorities visit 'community centres and care homes' to seek older residents' views.

evidence.quote: visiting community centres and care homes

questions.text: Which paragraph argues that designing for older people improves a park for everyone?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 says designing for the old 'tends to benefit everyone else as well'.

evidence.quote: tends to benefit everyone else as well

questions.text: Which paragraph describes the park as a setting for everyday social contact?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 calls the park 'a stage for ordinary social life'.

evidence.quote: a stage for ordinary social life

questions.text: Which paragraph suggests a city's values are revealed by the spaces it builds?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 states 'A society reveals its values in the spaces it builds'.

evidence.quote: A society reveals its values in the spaces it builds

questions.text: Which paragraph explains why older people's preferences may not be heard?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 notes older people are 'less likely to attend the public meetings' where decisions are made.

evidence.quote: less likely to attend the public meetings

questions.text: What single thing do studies link to a lack of contact for someone who lives alone?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 identifies 'isolation' as the chief risk for older people who rarely speak to anyone.

evidence.quote: is not physical frailty but isolation

sections.title: Passage 2 — Rebuilding the Reef
sections.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 14–26.
sections.passage:
Rebuilding the Reef

¶1 Coral reefs occupy a tiny fraction of the ocean floor, yet they shelter perhaps a quarter of all marine species and feed many millions of people. They are also among the ecosystems most exposed to a warming world. When the water around a reef grows too hot, the corals expel the microscopic algae that live within their tissues and supply most of their food, turning a brilliant white in a process known as bleaching. A bleached coral is not yet dead, but it is starving, and if the heat persists it will not recover. As such events grow more frequent, scientists who once merely studied reefs have found themselves trying to save them. The field that has grown up in response, usually called reef restoration, is young, inventive and acutely aware of how much is at stake.

¶2 The most established rescue technique borrows an idea from gardening. Fragments are broken from healthy corals and fixed to underwater frames or ropes, where they grow far faster than they would on the open seabed, sheltered from predators and competition. Once they reach a useful size, divers transplant them onto a damaged reef, much as a gardener moves seedlings from a nursery to a flower bed. Thousands of corals can be raised this way each year at a single site, and the method has restored visible patches of reef that had been reduced to rubble. Its great limitation is scale: the world's reefs cover hundreds of thousands of square kilometres, and no team of divers can plant more than a sliver of that by hand. For this reason, nursery work is increasingly seen not as a cure in itself but as a way of keeping options open while larger solutions are sought.

¶3 To raise corals worth planting, researchers must first understand which ones will endure. Not all corals are equally vulnerable to heat. On almost every reef a few colonies survive conditions that kill their neighbours, and these tough individuals have become the focus of intense interest. By breeding from such survivors, scientists hope to produce a generation of corals better able to withstand the temperatures the ocean is expected to reach. The approach is controversial. Critics warn that selecting for heat tolerance might sacrifice other qualities, and that human breeding could never keep pace with the speed at which the climate is changing in any case. Even its supporters concede that selective breeding can only ever form one part of a much wider response.

¶4 Knowing where to act at all depends on being able to watch the reef, and here technology has transformed what is possible. Satellites now track sea-surface temperatures across whole oceans, allowing scientists to predict a bleaching event weeks before it strikes and to warn the managers of vulnerable reefs. Closer in, autonomous underwater vehicles glide along the seabed, photographing the coral in such detail that software can later count colonies and measure their growth. Even sound is used: a healthy reef is surprisingly noisy, alive with the snapping and grunting of its inhabitants, and underwater microphones can gauge a reef's condition from its acoustic signature alone. Together these tools have turned reef monitoring from a matter of occasional dives into something close to continuous observation.

¶5 That last discovery has inspired one of the field's more imaginative experiments. Researchers found that the larvae of many reef creatures, drifting in the open water, use sound to locate a place to settle, steering towards the busy clamour of a thriving reef. On a degraded reef, which falls silent as its residents disappear, these larvae may simply drift past. By installing loudspeakers that broadcast the recorded sounds of a healthy reef, scientists have been able to lure young fish back to damaged areas, accelerating their natural recovery. The reef, in effect, is tricked into advertising a vitality it does not yet possess. The method works because sound, unlike light, travels far and fast through water, reaching drifting larvae long before any reef comes into view.

¶6 None of these methods, however, addresses the underlying problem. Restoration can repair a reef damaged by a storm or a single hot summer, but it cannot protect one from an ocean that keeps growing warmer year after year. Replanted corals will bleach again if the heat returns, and louder speakers cannot summon fish to water that has become uninhabitable. Almost every scientist working in the field is careful to make this point: their work buys time, it does not buy a solution. Reef restoration is best understood as emergency care for a patient whose underlying illness only the reduction of greenhouse gases can cure. Restoration without emissions cuts, in this sense, is a bucket bailing out a boat that is still taking on water.

¶7 Why, then, persist? The answer is partly practical and partly a matter of principle. Saving even fragments of reef preserves the species and the genetic variety from which a fuller recovery might one day be launched, should the climate eventually stabilise. It also sustains the communities, from fishing villages to dive operators, whose livelihoods would collapse with the reef. Beyond these calculations lies a simple refusal to give up on one of the planet's richest and most beautiful ecosystems. The work may be a holding action, its champions concede, but a holding action can still be worth the fight. To abandon the reef now, they argue, would be to lose for good what might one day be recovered.

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 1
options: New ways of watching the reef
Calling the fish back with sound
Treating the symptom, not the disease
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 establishes reefs as rich but 'among the ecosystems most exposed to a warming world', explaining bleaching.

evidence.quote: among the ecosystems most exposed to a warming world

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 2
options: New ways of watching the reef
Calling the fish back with sound
Treating the symptom, not the disease
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 describes the nursery method, which 'borrows an idea from gardening', moving fragments like seedlings.

evidence.quote: borrows an idea from gardening

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 3
options: New ways of watching the reef
Calling the fish back with sound
Treating the symptom, not the disease
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 explains breeding from heat-tolerant survivors to make corals that can 'withstand the temperatures the ocean is expected to reach'.

evidence.quote: better able to withstand the temperatures the ocean is expected to reach

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 4
options: New ways of watching the reef
Calling the fish back with sound
Treating the symptom, not the disease
correct_answer: New ways of watching the reef
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 surveys monitoring tools — satellites, underwater vehicles and microphones — that have 'transformed what is possible'.

evidence.quote: here technology has transformed what is possible

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 5
options: New ways of watching the reef
Calling the fish back with sound
Treating the symptom, not the disease
correct_answer: Calling the fish back with sound
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 describes broadcasting reef sounds through loudspeakers to 'lure young fish back to damaged areas'.

evidence.quote: lure young fish back to damaged areas

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 6
options: New ways of watching the reef
Calling the fish back with sound
Treating the symptom, not the disease
correct_answer: Treating the symptom, not the disease
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 stresses restoration 'buys time, it does not buy a solution' and cannot fix a warming ocean.

evidence.quote: their work buys time, it does not buy a solution

questions.text: Coral bleaching happens when corals expel the algae that normally provide their
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says the expelled algae 'supply most of their food', so bleaching removes their food source.

evidence.quote: supply most of their food

questions.text: The main limitation of the coral nursery method is that
options: it cannot be done at sufficient scale
it is illegal in most countries
correct_answer: it cannot be done at sufficient scale
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says its 'great limitation is scale' — divers can plant only a sliver of the world's reefs.

evidence.quote: Its great limitation is scale

questions.text: Why are some coral colonies of special interest to scientists?
options: they survive conditions that kill their neighbours
correct_answer: they survive conditions that kill their neighbours
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says a few colonies 'survive conditions that kill their neighbours' and so attract intense interest.

evidence.quote: survive conditions that kill their neighbours

questions.text: How do the larvae of many reef creatures find a place to settle?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 says the larvae 'use sound to locate a place to settle', steering towards a noisy reef.

evidence.quote: use sound to locate a place to settle

questions.text: Satellites help by tracking sea-surface ______ across whole oceans.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 says satellites 'track sea-surface temperatures across whole oceans' to predict bleaching.

evidence.quote: track sea-surface temperatures across whole oceans

questions.text: Scientists describe reef restoration as emergency ______ rather than a cure.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 calls restoration 'emergency care for a patient' whose real illness only emissions cuts can cure.

evidence.quote: emergency care for a patient

questions.text: Which TWO reasons does the final paragraph give for continuing restoration work?
options: it preserves species and genetic variety for a future recovery
it sustains communities whose livelihoods depend on the reef
it removes the need to cut emissions
correct_answer: it preserves species and genetic variety for a future recovery
it sustains communities whose livelihoods depend on the reef
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 says the work 'preserves the species and the genetic variety' and 'sustains the communities ... whose livelihoods would collapse with the reef'.

evidence.quote: preserves the species and the genetic variety
sustains the communities

sections.title: Passage 3 — How Expertise Is Built
sections.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 27–40.
sections.passage:
How Expertise Is Built

¶1 We tend to speak of expertise as though it were a gift. A grandmaster who reads a chessboard at a glance, a doctor who names a rare disease from across the room, a musician whose fingers seem to think for themselves: each is commonly described as a natural, as if their skill had been granted rather than earned. Decades of research into how experts actually develop tell a markedly different story. Talent, where it exists, sets a starting point, but the gulf between the competent and the truly exceptional is opened above all by the quantity and, crucially, the quality of practice. The romantic image of the effortless natural, it turns out, obscures the years of grinding, deliberate effort that almost always lie behind a great performance. Studies that have followed musicians, athletes and chess players over many years keep arriving at the same unglamorous conclusion.

¶2 Not all practice is equal. Simply repeating an activity one can already perform, however many hours are logged, produces little improvement once a comfortable level has been reached; the amateur golfer who plays every weekend for twenty years rarely ends up much better than after the first two. What distinguishes the expert is a particular and rather uncomfortable kind of training, in which the learner works at the very edge of their ability, attempting tasks just beyond reliable reach and attending closely to what goes wrong. This deliberate practice is effortful, often unpleasant, and demands intense concentration, which is one reason so few people sustain it. It is the difference between playing a piece one has already mastered and slowly, painfully fixing the single bar that always falls apart.

¶3 Feedback is its essential companion. To improve, a learner must know, quickly and accurately, whether each attempt succeeded or failed, and why. Where feedback is clear and immediate, as in chess or competitive sport, skill tends to climb steeply; where it is delayed, ambiguous or absent, even long experience may yield little. This helps explain a finding that surprises many: in certain professions, practitioners with twenty years behind them perform no better, and occasionally worse, than those with five, because the job never tells them plainly when their judgement has been wrong. Without that signal, mistakes are simply repeated until they harden into habit, and confidence grows even as accuracy does not. The lesson is a sobering one: experience teaches nothing unless the world, or a teacher, is willing to correct it.

¶4 As skill deepens, something curious happens to knowledge itself: it sinks out of conscious reach. The novice driver thinks explicitly about every movement, while the veteran changes gear without a flicker of attention, freeing the mind for the road ahead. Psychologists describe this as the development of automaticity, and it is what allows the expert nurse to sense that a patient is deteriorating before any single reading looks alarming. Such intuition can feel almost magical, yet it is not. It is the residue of thousands of past cases, compressed by experience into a swift, wordless recognition that the expert cannot fully explain even to themselves. What looks from the outside like a flash of insight is in truth a memory, retrieved so quickly that it never reaches the surface of awareness.

¶5 This silent, intuitive knowledge is among the expert's greatest assets, but it carries a hidden cost. Because the reasoning behind it is invisible, even to its owner, it is extraordinarily difficult to pass on. A master craftsman may genuinely wish to teach and still be unable to say what their hands are doing, which is why apprenticeship, slow imitation over years rather than instruction in weeks, remains the way many high skills are still transmitted. The very compression that makes expertise efficient also makes it stubbornly resistant to being written down. A textbook can capture the rules of a craft, but rarely the judgement that tells a master when to break them.

¶6 Intuition has a darker side as well, one that has occupied researchers for decades. The rapid pattern-matching that serves an expert so well within their field can mislead them disastrously outside it, or when conditions quietly change. Professor Daniel Aksoy has shown that expert intuition is reliable only in environments that are stable enough for genuine patterns to form and that provide the clear feedback by which they are learned. In a world that is irregular or rapidly shifting, he argues, the confident hunch of a veteran may be worth no more than a novice's guess, and is considerably more dangerous because it is trusted. The same hunch that saves a life in a familiar emergency may cost one when the situation merely appears familiar. Recognising the boundary of one's own competence is, on this view, itself a mark of genuine expertise.

¶7 The practical implications reach well beyond any single craft. If expertise is built rather than bestowed, then the right kind of training matters more than the search for natural talent, and organisations that wish to develop skilled people should engineer the conditions, demanding tasks and honest feedback, under which skill actually grows. Dr Priya Raman adds an important qualification: societies should also learn to ask where a particular expert's intuition can be trusted and where it cannot, rather than treating expertise as a single quality that, once earned, applies everywhere. To understand how expertise is built is, in the end, to understand both its remarkable power and its precise limits. Expertise, properly understood, is neither a miracle to be revered nor a myth to be debunked, but a human achievement that can be cultivated wherever the conditions are right. That is a more hopeful message than the myth of talent it replaces, for it places excellence, at least in principle, within far more people's reach.

questions.text: The writer believes expertise is mainly an inborn gift.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says the gap to excellence is opened 'above all by the quantity and ... quality of practice', not by a gift.

evidence.quote: the quantity and, crucially, the quality of practice

questions.text: Simply repeating a familiar activity brings little improvement after a certain point.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says repeating what one can already do 'produces little improvement once a comfortable level has been reached'.

evidence.quote: produces little improvement once a comfortable level has been reached

questions.text: Deliberate practice is usually enjoyable and relaxing.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 calls deliberate practice 'effortful, often unpleasant', contradicting 'enjoyable and relaxing'.

evidence.quote: effortful, often unpleasant

questions.text: Chess players earn more money than other kinds of expert.
questions.explanation: Chess is mentioned as an example of clear feedback, but the passage says nothing about experts' earnings, so this is Not Given.

questions.text: In some professions, people with much more experience perform no better than less experienced colleagues.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says practitioners with twenty years 'perform no better, and occasionally worse, than those with five' where feedback is poor.

evidence.quote: perform no better, and occasionally worse, than those with five

questions.text: The writer concludes that the right training matters more than searching for natural talent.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 says if expertise is built, 'the right kind of training matters more than the search for natural talent'.

evidence.quote: the right kind of training matters more than the search for natural talent

questions.text: According to the passage, what most distinguishes expert practice from ordinary repetition?
options: working at the edge of one's ability
correct_answer: working at the edge of one's ability
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says the expert 'works at the very edge of their ability', attempting tasks just beyond reach.

evidence.quote: works at the very edge of their ability

questions.text: Skill improves most steeply when feedback is
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says where feedback is 'clear and immediate ... skill tends to climb steeply'.

evidence.quote: clear and immediate

questions.text: The process by which an expert acts without conscious attention is called
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 says psychologists call this 'the development of automaticity'.

evidence.quote: the development of automaticity

questions.text: Why is expert intuition so hard to teach?
options: the reasoning behind it is invisible even to its owner
correct_answer: the reasoning behind it is invisible even to its owner
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 says intuition is hard to pass on because 'the reasoning behind it is invisible, even to its owner'.

evidence.quote: the reasoning behind it is invisible, even to its owner

questions.text: Expert intuition is reliable only in stable environments that give clear feedback.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6: Aksoy showed intuition is reliable only where environments are 'stable enough for genuine patterns to form' with clear feedback.

evidence.quote: stable enough for genuine patterns to form

questions.text: We should ask where a particular expert's intuition can be trusted and where it cannot.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7: Raman adds that societies should 'ask where a particular expert's intuition can be trusted and where it cannot'.

evidence.quote: ask where a particular expert's intuition can be trusted and where it cannot

questions.text: Within a stable field, expert intuition is essentially the compressed residue of thousands of past ______.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 calls intuition 'the residue of thousands of past cases'.

evidence.quote: the residue of thousands of past cases

questions.text: Because expert knowledge is hard to put into words, many high skills are still passed on through ______.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 says this is why 'apprenticeship ... remains the way many high skills are still transmitted'.

evidence.quote: apprenticeship
